So here's what i would love some inputs on. 
We've been doing centralized and tops down quarterly planning so far. Worked when the company was smaller. but at our current pace of growth, this is no longer feasible. We want to move to a continuous backlog process across the entire org, where dependency management can happen pretty seamlessly. But we also want to have some light weight planning around quarterly boundaries to finalize the next Q's roadmaps. What are some of the processes and tools that have really worked for you? We are in a very regulated industry, so there'll be a lot of compliance/legal related asks that have to be serviced every Q.
here a summary of what we implemented across 17 scrum/kanban teams
unified backlog, basically all the features / work... along with defect 'buckets' of time basically... in excel, with a lot of columns for specific needs/team assignments/tshirt sizes
visualized w/ miro, broken out by sprint / quarter per team to highlight the major work the team is taking
use azure devops to track/manage the actual work during sprints
as part of the backlog, we have release ahead planning (rap), where the stakeholders (across 20ish project) meet weekly to review our current quarter + next for priority calls and changes
we have a backlog health metric for upcoming work... basically, 'do we know enough about it to write a user story' and 'has a story been made' to ensure we're giving our teams sprint-able work
awesome. how does dependency management work?

we have one team that manages alot of the specific config work that hasn't been distilled into teams yet, we do 'pint' (e.g. 6 weeks / 3 sprint) in a big room planning (but we're not sAFE)... so we try to make any dependency needs known there
we host weekly scrum of scrums, mainly what do you need from other teams, and what are you finishing up this week
several years ago we did the big brown paper wallboard with yard to show dependency, like Henrik from lego did here: https://crisp.se/wp-content/uploads/2016/12/Agile@Lego.pdf

I have a continuous process:
1) quarterly project prioritization and leads assignment.
2) monthly task grooming, duration estimates, scheduling, task priorities, incoming asks from partners. 
3) weekly progress reviews and risk management
4) daily kanban to assign tasks and tech consults.
gotcha. I need to scale this across what is now a 2000 member product/eng/design org.

We've done a few quarterly planning sessions remote, in person will always triumph imo but you can't beat the cost and logistical savings 😄  Agenda is usually laid out as 3 days (2-3 hours) of PMs presenting their roadmaps where everyone is invited. First day is reviewing prior quarter, second day is rough draft, third is final draft. 

After roadmap sharing is done,  teams split to refine their epics/features and build roadmaps.

@AG can I DM you
I'll start with copy pasting one of my responses to a blind question. Even tho this was specific to FB, the underlying principles still apply ( I think)

1/ give yourself some breathing space; there'll be a lot of smart people around.. it is ok to feel overwhelmed, but remember to cut yourself some slack. You are there because you cleared a tough set of interviews, so you def deserve the seat at the table. 
2/ FB is very impact driven; once you get settled, ask the hard questions about your programs - what impact is it driving, what will your role be. what can you uniquely bring to the table - maybe a PoV that can help teams think about the impact/goals in a different manner. 
3/ TPMs at FB are a lot about building relationships, navigating formal and informal reporting structures, influencing without authority, etc. Create a stakeholder map so you know which relationships you need to build and nurture on a constant basis. 
4/ understand your product/program areas well. People are usually very generous with their time, but do your own homework -- read up on wikis/quips/gdocs/wp docs before you setup 1-1s and try and understand someone's role, area of expertise, etc. 
5/ internalize the TPM career framework and the expectations for your level. Constantly calibrate with your manager how you are trending; first half can feel a bit choppy, so asking for early feedback and constantly adjusting is key. At the very least it will avoid the surprise come perf review time. 
6/ find yourself a mentor/buddy if your manager hasn't already allocated one for you. Also, as you start building trust in the org, also try and find yourself a workspouse - someone you can really use as a sounding board, but won't be judgemental.
7/ start contributing to TPM org wide stuff; this is important for perf reviews, but is also super helpful to build your network outside of your core team.
Thanks @AG

Now, generalizing this a bit more.. 
Understand the culture of the org and what the expectations of the tpm role are.. Are there good TPMs you can emulate of even shadow? Can you get 1-2 as mentors. 
2/ understanding the system : after the first 1-1 with your eng, draw the high level architecture of your product area.. in every subsequent conversation, use that as a starting point, but build on it with info the new person is giving. Finally, present it back to the core teams at the end of your first/second month.. no greater test of learning that trying to teach it all back.
As a TPM, do you choose an execution methodology or you just go ahead with what the team has been doing?

I would assess what the team is doing/following and what the pitfalls are. If things are working fine, best not to change things out. 
If they aren't working fine, address the gaps that'll drive the most impact. 
Depending on the appetite for change on the team, see if you want to rollout something big bang or take a more measured "piloting" approach. 
Try and understand if they've worked with TPMs before and what +ve and -ve experiences they've had. And try to calibrate based on that.
Yes, thanks!
Other folks - please chime in with your thoughts. And what has worked for you. 🙏🏻

I don’t see a reason to change the ways of working drastically if the team is performing ok. That does not mean there’s nothing to do. Every team has pain-points in terms of processes or impediments to resolve. Talk to the team and stakeholders and figure out which are the high-priority improvements to address. I don’t think there’s one specific way to address all those improvements.

I’d follow the existing method until I fully understand the pros/cons of it.
I’ve seen people going into an org immediately trying to change things, and it usually backfires.

Agree - have worked with some eng teams that consider TPM as someone  just interested in  timelines - and have faced resistance .
Helps to take a piloting approach in such cases - where the pilot team adopts new processes and frameworks. Results can be your proof to drive the change

First for me is to understand WHIM (what's it in for me), this case them... who are you reporting too, what's important to them, and if something is green/yellow/red status, what actions are you/project taking to upgrade it to the next status in a short summary

Also, when you start or take over a project... it might sound old school, but a stakeholder management plan, is something really helpful... it doesn't have to be anything official and something you don't even share... but it's something you create to understand who's who, what are they doing for the project, what do they need from you, how often do you need to communicate/update them, etc...

Then communications style, if you are reporting to find out style of communication they do prefer... is there a monthly/week progress review they want to be part of, an email summary, slack updates, in person/zoom updates etc..  Be flexible to what suits them, especially the more senior the folks you working with.  All things you likely know already

In this job function, do you spend a lot of time just getting, what seem to be, the same updates regarding the dev items? For instance, we have 10 items we are tracking for our release. I feel like more than 5 times a week, 5 of us meet to discuss the status of how we are tracking on these items and what dates can we meet. This is in addition to our daily standup with the engineers as well. It seems a little crazy. Like have a date, plan for some slippage due to unknowns, requirements flexibility, but deliver on or close to it. Why should we constantly have to discuss the damn dates

And how would you deal with this situation: I’ve been on this project for about 3-4 months. There is a group of engineers that I interface with on a daily basis. One engineer in particular takes whatever I say, and seems to double check with my boss. Sometimes he bypasses me altogether and when I asked my manager, he said he was talking to him about something and the eng just asked him a question so he responded. He didn’t mean to leave me out (I believe him). One particular time, I was giving a decision to this eng and another one and they just wouldn’t listen to me. So I had my manager join the call who said the same thing I did. Afterwards, I pulled the eng aside and asked him how I could have phrased things differently or presented them differently and he said it wasn’t him, it was the other guy. So I said ok.

Today, we had a discussion, I gave him a direction and then in our weekly status meeting, he asks my manager about the same topic I already have him an answer on. My manager is unaware we discussed this already and gives him the same answer I did
To me, I am wondering if this is because I’m a female and he is a young Indian male? Or am I just inept. I don’t think this is the case because none of the other engineers seem to do this to me
I do know that I need to nip this in the bud. Do I talk to him again and his manager?
Apologies if this is the wrong channel but I thought it has to do with how do TPMs function

Calls

@anon123  just speaking out loud here... I wonder if he is a bit anxious/nervous to talk to you directly?
if you think that might be the case, then definitely good to ask him about that in the next 1-1

@anon123 - Female here - I don’t think this is about you / your gender.
Couple things  could be in the play -
1. Some people have the tendency to double check everything with the next  in the hierarchy chain-  accepting decisions coming from someone higher in hierarchy makes it easier for them - it’s a culture trait often in people with Asian upbringing . Again , am generalizing here .
2. He probably is not comfortable approaching you multiple times to double click on the same issue - hence reaches out to your boss

encourage you to talk to your peer and see if having slack/email convos help ease the strain( if any - between the both of you) 
Have your boss come and be present for a few meetings while you’re driving and delivering critical program info/decisions. Also request your boss to redirect all questions on the project to you - he can be cc’ed in the convo .That should convey the message that you’re in charge

@anon123 - Yes. In my org..we have TPMs and PMTs. Even though the roles/responsibilities are intertwined the fundamental difference is that PMT owns the product roadmap and TPM owns the delivery aspect
Who writes feature ac and who participates in the retro?

1) user story creation, acceptance criteria is defined by PMT
2) delivery plan, scrums, retros, status calls (we have weekly business review, monthly business review, quarterly business review) are run by TPM. Retros are attended by the cross functional TPM/PMT and SDM for large programs. 
3) In some smaller programs/teams, i have seen TPM or PMT owing both.  And, retros are attended by the sprint team and they happen more frequently (every sprint or 2 sprints) depending on team's appetite

I realize i come  from smaller companies and I did run agile teams in multiple capacities: scrum master, dev manager, program manager before Microsoft. I always did force Eng to go deeper and also take accountability in their retro. As my dad likes to say, we clap with two hands. So it shouldn’t be a blame game and it shouldn’t be one sided.
It feels like it and I’m trying to distance myself from this and take it with a grain of salt but if the team isn’t introspective, it’s going to be tough to get better

@anon123 - You brought up multiple use-cases. i will try to break into the below points
1) Retrospective - Each team (engineering or test engineering or UX) could have their own retrospectives with the intent of improving the individual team productivity. When it comes to project or program retrospective, it should be a cross-functional team effort with the primary intent to make program/project successful
2) User stories - The business feature stories should come from product owner (the role can be done by engineering manager or TPM too). But, there should a Single threaded product owner to define product roadmap, prioritization and acceptance criteria. For technical stories (perf improvements, upgrades etc), the user stories should come from single threaded tech leader. The sprint planning should be driven by a single thread program leader (TPM) to balance between business stories/tech stories based on program prioritization
3) Burden of the past to future outlook - The team has had some history (good or bad) with previous program managers.  Easy to say than done..but i advise to take small steps. try to join the flow of the team and establish trust. Be a hands-on leader by working with engineers (and ux, qa etc). And, then come up with a strategy to run efficient retros

I have worked at multiple medium and large companies (Amazon is the largest in my career. Each company had a different flavor of the same problem. And, there is no single solution to solve it. In your specific case, my recommendation is to have a 1:1 with engineering manager to have a candid discussion on how you can help the team to remove impediments and make progress. If you are going above and beyond your role (which you are), make it clear to your manager in your 1:1 (if you do not have 1:1, please schedule one. i cannot emphasize on this for a new employee) and repeat it in every meeting.

How is engineering held responsible for misses that could have been avoided due to forethought? The key is to have accountability so that it’s painful for them not to put in the effort. I would also suggest accounting time in advance for the things that you know will come up because of past experiences. I would also enlist the help of some people who also wanted deeper discussions in the beginning. During planning I would remind them of things that came up previously that they didn’t account for. I took the let me help you plan so that as a team, we make our dates. I kept stressing we succeed and fail as a team. For a reluctant crew, it took some time but I won them over eventually.

messages.content: What are some tips and techniques to dealing with customers/stakeholders who constantly complain. Almost like making a mountain out of a mole hole? I recently started in a new role where things are in a flux, I know it will take some time for things to stabilize and I am still learning a lot of new things.

Document your side, have them confirm. Tell them this is your path to resolution and if they don’t agree, have them tell you now. record meetings if possible. Introduce the concept that both your team and their team are human and accountable to the same rules. If they want mountains, they need to accept the mountains they want.

As someone who is in a customer facing role, usually when they constantly complain it is because they have certain expectations that are not being met, whether or not you agree with them is a different matter.  Can you clarify what areas that are unhappy with?  Deliverables not meeting requirements, quality of deliverables, communication, something else?  It is important to know what they are expecting or looking for as different problems have different solutions.  I have turned around many relationships just by understanding their problems and building structure and organization to deliver solutions.  I have had customers that ultimately felt as though there wasn't enough transparency during program execution and some customers that wanted to really deep dive into technical aspects.  If you are in a new role, it makes it easier to play the new guy card and just flat out ask the concerns that they have so that you can work to address them.

In general though: 
1. As Excel said, documentation is your friend.  Clearly document scope, SOW, etc.  There could be different ways to interpret customer problems into a solution.  Our company also develops a wireframe and user flow before starting development to take any additional ambiguity out of the end deliverable.
2. Document every CR, big or small.  I let our commercial team be the judge if there is a CR worth billing (some customers may not like being nickled and dimed), but keeping history here is important.
3. Document all decisions made, send out clear meeting notes.  Attendees, items discussed, decisions made, etc.

You gotta make sure your manager is in your side. Document the feedback and in your next 1-1 with your manager, ask them what is reasonable and what feedback you need to prioritize. Then go back to your stakeholders and say what you will address based on that conversation. If your manager says, “address everything now”, then that is a major red flag and you should start looking for a new one asap.
Be the messenger between various parties in the beginning and don't throw in too many personal opinions when you are still learning the facts/dynamics. And don't take anything they say personal.

It also may be worthwhile understanding if you can set into place some frameworks for setting clearer expectations. 

I am in a product company in a client-facing role, so I know this all too well. 

One of example of a process that has made a huge difference to the challenge of clients throwing tons of work at us was to educate them on planning and roadmap development.

I show them our team's capacity and how that flows into our roadmap planning, and we mutually align on workstreams for the next quarter. Of course, things may arise, but we can generally point to our roadmap and ask them to prioritize tradeoffs.

Similarly, if we're working on an implementation, I make sure up front to overemphasize budget, scope and ensure they know that THEY are responsible for any overages caused by them. Weekly, I'll provide status updates letting them know of timeline/ budget risks. This is all very PM 101, but I do believe it makes a huge difference to be overly structured with these types of clients if there is any chance their complaints arise from a lack of understanding about what you can reasonably deliver.

Reading Chi's answer above, I think this aligns with the points already made. 

If it's not a matter of expectations not being met, but rather just tough personalities, the dale carnegie classic How to Make Friends has some great reminders of how to hear people out, show empathy, build relationships, etc

I’ve ran several large steerco type meetings and there are a few things I’ve found that minimizes the pain to some extent. Having a solid agenda is foremost but beyond that:
1. Having a consistent format and location for agenda and notes. I don’t want to spam a large group, so I will just keep these artifacts on a confluence page which is well designed with different sections and keep updating a separate tab for each date. Only the link to the page is inside the meeting invite. Can be done using MS teams as well with a separate group. People who don’t attend can just go to that location to look at decisions. 
2.  Use adobe web conf or something stable for screen share instead of Skype so people can self subscribe to the share. Use the chat feature to drive engagement by inviting folks to type their inputs into the chat.  Helps keep people awake when they have live engagement. Put the onus on action owners or action item creators to write down the action item or decision in the chat. That way you don’t have to pull your hair out trying to understand highly technical stuff and record it. Chat transcript can go right where you store the meeting notes.

We have something called as the “Operational Product Plan” (OPP). This lists the goals and OKRs at the org level. The TPMs take that and map goals/OKRs to our business unit/vertical. Then we come up with prioritized list of features/projects/initiatives (sorted above the line/below the line). We then make resourcing asks (Dev, QA, PM, Design etc)  based on 70-80% guesstimate. Our VP takes the final call on resourcing and comes back with the number. Then based on this we update the roadmap.
How do we usually manage dependency and trade off in a program? If there a process or framework that we use?

This depends on the type of programs. If it’s a big bet initiative the budget is already allocated at org or corporate level and TPMs don’t worry about it. Product defines what is the MVP and what needs to be  EAC/GA.  To make this happen we work with Engineering to get their estimates . And PM defines the  product roadmap.  (There is a interview question - how  you TPM influenced the product strategy).

Dependency - we create XP(Cross Pillar ticket. If you use Jira…) and assign to the xfn team that needs to address/deliver this. And you request them to provide a fixed version ( i..e which sprint they cans deliver this) . This is one way you can define dependency and track it.  If the xfn team cannot deliver in the sprint you need then we bring the product and have the trade off discussion, to deliver this in the next internal release. (For example).
Also you report this in your steering co(with product and engineering leadership) and also MOR (Monthly Operational Review).
The charm of using XP in Jira is you can pull a report  and present to audience and show where you have a commitment problem from Xfn

Hi all, I'm trying to understand the TPM role at Tech companies, particularly for product. For example, I initially became interested in a recruiter reaching out to apply to a TPM jobs at Facebook Reality Labs, and am now just trying to learn more.

For background, I have ~11 YOE and have been a "TPM" for 2.5 years now at a company delivering physical products (hardware with embedded software). From a technical point of view the hardware bit is ok because it feels "real", but comes with a lot of problems and constraints, so I'd like to diversify more and do either software products or lead a software component of a physical product.  

After doing some research, my initial impression is that there is much more consistent image for Product Management and Engineering, while TPM varies from "Chief scheduler" to "the glue that holds it all together and is the real hero", or something like that. I have enough experience to know that most roles are what you make of it is, but I don't have a good feel of how to figure out what the balance is for TPMs at these companies (FAANG and the nearby tiers).

To be a bit more specific - I feel like ultimately I want a job that is split 70% TPM and 30% Prod. Manager. 

Why? I really like being a TPM because I enjoy being a team lead and motivating people to kick ass. If I didn't have a strong connection to the execution, I'd probably get too lost in my head and lose interest because I'd feel disconnected from making the product vision a reality.

I also like to be involved with the business case/long term vision/requirements for our product at some level. At my current company there is grey area so sometimes I'm shoulder-to-shoulder with Strategy engaging with the customer to get feedback, or discovering requirements with them directly. It helps me feel motivated to know that I'm aligned with the product vision and it makes it a lot easier to do my job as a TPM. I also have an MBA and just genuinely enjoy the business stuff.

So anyways - Long story short, do product TPM jobs at Tech companies have that split of Execution and Product Strategy? Is that more likely to be in a PM role that has TPM duties? What should I be looking for in job openings, or what questions should I ask to find out?
Find a PM role in a product area that doesn't have a TPM. :)

Microsoft PM jobs are usually that way, so it will be a great fit for you. At Amazon, it can be that way if you are lucky as it is very decentralized. At Google, you will be far from product involvement. At Meta, from what I read, product TPM jobs can be close to what you are looking for but still not exactly what you are looking for.

Hi folks, 

I’m looking to pick some of your brains. When setting up a new program what are a few things to consider when organizing work streams? I want each workstream to have a consistent set of deliverable types over time and create a sense of a single team. On the other hand I don’t want to create silos… I’m sure there some other things to consider, dependency minimization, SLAs… maybe I’m complicating things too. 🙂

@Pebkac .. I am almost in the same boat . Will be starting a new program soon . Yet to figure out how I can support them . I am considering identifying the gaps and then accordingly may be set up processes like weekly sync , common dashboards to track progress and dependencies , RACI , stakeholder comms etc .
There’s a good idea! Mapping to XFN teams!

Depends on how many team members you deal with and if they work with each other on a regular basis, the model can be quite different.

In my experience, if there's already a set of teams that are familiar with their respective R&R, then you can set up the deliverables structurally and assign DRIs directly and just have weekly sync to go over high priority/high risk stuff.

If the formation is not clear and it requires a lot of back and forth to figure out who's doing what, then you might want to keep the high level goals visible but keep the tasks to yourself and call small team meetings to sort through them one by one.

Perhaps the best thing you could do now is creating a dependency map between teams by joining each team's scrums. Then do the homework and see the friction points (difference between expectations between teams, in terms of timeline). This is a very exhaustive process at least at Amazon, we get lost in dates and DFDs. 

Once you do this heavy lifting, then try mitigating by working with SDMs and tech leads. I come from a long background in a finance company, there, things are easier to manage, they follow SAFE, etc. But in big tech. Things are different, each team go at breakneck speed, very difficult for a SDM to keep track of various programs and their timelines. You come in to help the SDM prioritize each sprint's work. You need to do a lot of planning offline though.

The above is with the L6 TPM perspective. If you're L5, your area of operation is usually limited to one team and limited dependencies. There you need to do a lot of grassroots level work, compared to L6 TPM. You can then start with requirements, design, etc. Creating timeline, getting dates or date commitments from external teams. And creating milestones based on these inputs. A L5 TPM spends more time with dev team than a L6 TPM

Qq- if you’re planning to roll out a feature to let’s say UK, India and Germany. What are some of the assumptions, work streams needed, dependencies, teams impacted, major milestones, and the risks for the roll out?
Why don't you take a stab at answering this first and get people to give feedback on that answer?
From experience that’s definitely a heavy question

**I can give some high level details based on my work experience but leaving it to the group to chime in and provide feedback* *
1/Design & Plan -  Design the functionality and detail the user-case. Work with Product managers & UX. Plan for A/B scenarios. sometimes, you may need to target the customers/regions. 
2/ Plan timeline/launch date for the rollout. If you are planning for global product, you may need to involve marketing partners, notifications (email support team) and others workstream teams as needed
3/ Develop - You can do your own strategy to build the feature depends on how your org is structured. Main thing is you need to build using "feature toggle" (if you are mentioning feature toggle - sometimes expect some good questions from interviewer like why, what, how around feature toggles.) 
4/ Testing - testing is key here. you need to give ample time for testing. For product launch having global impact, always test using features toggles. 
5/Deploy to prod - Usually critical features always rollout as DARK mode (OFF) in prod and incrementally rollout to some set of customers/regions. test the functionality to see if it is working. if all goes well, increment the trottle to 100% live for all customers/regions. If something goes wrong, you do have the feature toggle to turn OFF instead of backing out the entire code.
Additionally you can also add after rollout "Gathering user feedback like VOC channel or some sort of feedback that u have in our application to get customer feedback.
finally, plan for incremental changes based on customer feedback or optimization your product needs. 
This is one of the Hit or miss question where you can easily score in the interview but make sure you are presenting gracefully instead of reading it from the book. Interviewers in FB, G are very smart to catch you if you don't have working experience!

Recently I have been working on platform programs.

Note: I am a little biased towards applications, so take my comments with a grain of salt

I see the below Pros and Cons of the platform team

Pros

- In general, initiatives can have a positive impact on the whole company
- More technical and cutting edge, platform teams need to be in the bleeding edge

Cons

- Some of my senior executives care more about applications, growth ..etc. I see application programs get a lot more executive attention than platform programs
- If you break something, the whole world goes down (minor changes can have a major impact)
- Getting adoption is a drag; let's say the platform team introduces x functionality, then getting it implemented by the application teams requires a lot of coordination and time

Usually in true bottoms up fashion, product teams figure out what to build to align with the high level goals/OKRs that are set at an overall product level. the teams then look at what goals and KPIs make sense to set and how they impact the overall goals. Leadership then typically uses this impact analysis to make prioritization decisions. for net new, 0-1 stuff, usually product and eng leaders together make an HC ask upwards. all of this is consolidated and presented to exec team a couple of times a year -- and a portion of that ask gets approved. It is then upon the product and eng leaders to determine what projects/initiatives they will fund and by how much. usually HC allocated is for eng, and then xfn hc is allocated as a % of that eng number. 
TPMs can influence this process in multiple instances -- validating LoEs, working with eng on assumptions, tradeoffs, dependencies with other teams, etc. and working with DS on setting the right goals and making sure line of sight is clear, etc.

In that case, you need to find as much information as possible. Find out all the stakeholders - who are the people that can give you the information. Here's a few other things you can do to identify the problem    ---  What is the hypothesis? You know there is some problem even if you don’t know everything about it. 
- Do I have all the data I need? Get all the facts together will help derive insights
- Who can give me more information? You need to talk to as many people as needed to get your data together and they can also help identify parts of the problem
- Who is the end user/customer? This helps frame the why this is a problem. And how many people does it impact?
- Why is this important to solve now? This helps with prioritization
- At this point you have clarify and potential solutions - which you can rank.

Approach - i would start by understanding  the goals, work with key stakeholders and prepare RACI so everyone is clear of roles and responsibilities. I got this recently and here is how I answered which is pretty similar to yours.... 

Review goals/objective, milestones, Success Criteria, Risks, and Dependencies

Review Sprint, and Release KPIs to identify improvement areas

Review backlog and team prioritization (feature intake vs technical improvement stories)

Review program communication (top, down, and sideways) to identify if there is need for better communication (this is usually underrated area and requires maximum efforts of TPM)

Work with cross functional teams to understand their perspective of how program is going and if they think they need help in any particular area

Are there any impediments which i can remove

Usually, team which is working well in X situation; might not work well in situation Y. Try to find those unknown events where I can help

Ended on lighter note --- if found no area where I can improve or help, that means team is high performing on auto pilot.. why to change something which is working!!! 🙂 I will follow the processes being followed so far

Once we are ready to start. Pilot environment simulating existing environment will be in place. We don’t have capacity to refresh data so we have data frozen in time. Users are given list of their usage pattern and are expected to test all aspects of system they use at high level initially. Big tracked, resolved parallely. Adjust system config as needed.Training session organized after users have initial feel of system. Then parallel full blown testing end to end based on whole business process which was developed in coordination with power users.  No capability here but big companies probably have ability to stress test. Once everything good . Assuming any metrics collected during piloting helps set up final production system. Go live. Resolve anything that comes up . But hopefully strong piloting helps avoid issue. Get user feedback of the experience. Note of own learning for future migration’s. Goal to improve from last time experience.

messages.content:
Hi all,
I’m looking for advice on communicating to executives, specifically via email. I’m working with a new stakeholder who wants to know the play by play. This is a bit new for me as I’m used to communicating with when there’s something more substantial. We have a program update that’s no-weekly, with all the information in @Priyanka’s communication template. The feedback specifically is that I’m not being transparent about my next steps. FYI, I don’t believe this is micromanagement. I think it’s a conflict between my need for autonomy and over communicating my thought process. Does any have any experience here? My simplest solution is to add: 1) here’s what we’re doing 2) here’s what we intend to do… I imagine there’s a better way… any help would be appreciated!

There's a section in the communication template called "What's Next / Priorities". This section is meant for your stakeholders to understand what's coming next.  Why do you think people ask for next steps. If you put yourself in their shoes, it will help. Not knowing something increases anxiety - for your stakeholders and even for management. The more they know things with certainty, the more it helps them communicate upwards. It also is helpful because they might have an idea that might speed things up for you. My suggestions - Add that section with clear steps on what is happening between now and the next report and where does it stand in priority. Also mention if something is trickly and needs help or even if there's a risk, you have it under control. When they know you are in control, they feel relieved and start trusting you more. HTH

One more thing - over communicating is always good even if its your thought process...but don't come across as too experimental or unsure of what your next steps are. You still need to demonstrate confidence and that if even if you don't know something, you have clear steps to help get clarity. You know similar to having a date for a date.

Thanks for your response. The “what’s happening between now and the next report” is valuable, as is the part about “helping others to communicate up”, and “demonstrating confidence by showing the steps to get clarity”. Thanks again!

I haven't seen the template @Priyanka referred to.. but we use "tl;dr", "going well" and "worried about" sections to call out key risks. And +1 to tpmify's call out on "what's next" -- ideally I like to see "plans for next 2 weeks" in a report. (and any mitigation steps for items in the "worried about" section.)
it may also be beneficial to see who else is sending reports to this leader and may NOT have gotten the same feedback as you. and see what they are doing differently.

Finally, about this _The feedback specifically is that I’m not being transparent about my next steps. FYI, I don’t believe this is micromanagement._ 
I think you are working off of assumptions and it might be best to clarify why this individual is asking for the detail. that way, your report can address the specific outcome he/she plans to drive with that info.
Very good insights. Appreciate the response.

hi everyone! Does anyone have any good resources (or perhaps personal examples to share) that concretely illustrate how to have influence and produce change program-wide?

 My manager said he'd love for me to begin identifying inefficiencies across the program across all projects. Sorry this question requires better context and maybe examples. In general ‘building trust’ is important before you influence a group of people. Your story to influence folks should also cover ‘what current problem your idea will solve’, ‘what’s the result/consequences of current problem’, ‘why teams need to try a new way’… any other alternates to address the problem- basically create a safe space where teams recognize the problem and agree as a group to try a new way to solve the problem - this way you get a buy in to implement your idea too if it’s a good one.
It would be critical to co-opt your key stakeholders into the solutioning process as well.

You got some great pointers from IceMan and AG. There isn't a formula to influencing and it does take time. You have to prioritize your work. What your manager is asking you to do is perhaps a way for you to grow in your role by having broader impact. Clarify the prioritization of your work and timeframe for implementing the change. messages.content: Hi folks looking to do annual planning at a small company ~250 with the executive team. We're planning on a multi-day onsite to work on this before sharing the results more broadly with the company. Any suggestions on structures or agendas you've found to be particularly successful? We're trying to balance discussions vs being too top down

**Framing 1: **
A leader/exec (CEO/head of prod or strategy) walks thru : 
Top of mind, 
State of the union, Key metrics, 
Market trends that they expect to impact the company 
Some sort of overview of 'Big Rocks' for 2023 

and then groups of folks brainstorm on each big rock area. come up with suggestions to win, BHAGs, product ideas, etc. 
potentially stack rank a list of ideas, create affinity groups, etc. --> build into a plan of action

**Framing 2 :** 
1/ Common ideology (30 mins)
2/ Rules of Engagement (5 mins)
  - Be respectful
  - Be transparent
  - Be inclusive
  - What says here stays here
  - Agree to disagree and commit
  - Any decision made is a team decision
  - Have a parking lot for non P0 items that need follow ups
3/ Why do we exist, what is our purpose (1 hour)
  - Current state, 
  - End state
4/ How are we set up currently and what are the challenges to get us to end state (1 hour)
5/ Vision Statement (1 hour)
  - Where should we be in X, Y, Z timeframe
  - Are we staffed for this
  - Create a vision statement
6/ What is short term plan (30 mins)
7/ What is long term plan (30 mins)

Great stuff by AG. One  thing I would add, which helps buy in and commitments is a “blind” confidence vote. We would cap our PI planning off with this at Disney. If the vote came back low, we did another round of discovery, dependency review, capacity etc. we re-worked the plan until we had a high confidence vote. “Blind” is the key though, you’d be surprised how many senior and even execs won’t speak up to risks and low confidence when in a room of peers who hate and or want to be done with planning and will accept a very flawed plan just to leave. Also, get great catering. Plenty of snacks and drinks. People feel the investment and will be more open to collaborate when fed and cared for.

also look for insights from Shreyas Doshi on Twitter/LI.. see tweet for an example : 
https://twitter.com/shreyas/status/1384008853004578822

messages.content: hi. Is there a framework to follow when company A bought company B and now needs to migrate the entire IT Infra to mirror company A?  There are a lot of unknown so I was thinking of obviously starting with discovery meetings

You’re definitely on a right path with discovery meetings. 

I’d read on migrations best practices, there was a post on Pragmatic Engineer blog and few other resources. 

Are A and B drastically different?

I have done something like that company A take over company B i was responsible for integration.  Before even starting with discovery phase try to put some quantifiable OKR right at the beginning of the program - reason being quantifiable numbers will help you measure success of the program at the end. Like number of overlapping tools etc. Also in general these programs are very high visibility so make sure you understand what your leadership wants and start from there.
Thanks for sharing. Just bought the book.

I have seen consolidation happening in phases. You will need leadership's input on the priorities. If the acquisition is large, then you may want to bring in a consulting firm to ramp up and execute the program. 

In general, you can follow the org chart to see what systems need to be consolidated. For example, if HR is merging to become one team, then HR systems need to be merged. If Customer Support remains separate, then those supporting systems can remain separate.

- Product level integrations (if you are a software company, like Adobe buying Figma), then you need to figure out what are the integrations between your products
- Identity is going to be key; what login will the acquired team use for authentication
- Figure out the core IT services that need to be consolidated first. This can be HR, Payroll, Active Directory,  Email, Office Suite , Slack/Teams, and some of the core systems
- Consolidation of data centers, networks, PC builds
- Consolidate any licenses (cloud services, software and others)
- Merger and Retirement of Business Applications (e.g. CRM, Procurement and others)
- Software development process consolidation (like security, development, architecture and others)

Moving the question from @pitbull11111 into here for visibility and feedback from the community.

One approach to mapping user stories to milestones is to break down big milestones into smaller pieces, such as Epics or Features. These intermediate-level pieces of work provide a link between the day-to-day efforts of the team and the larger milestone that may span over several months or quarters. By linking Epics and Features to milestones, you can better organize the work and ensure that the team is making progress towards achieving the overall objectives of the project.

Using tools like Jira, Azure DevOps, or other similar tools can also be helpful in this process. These tools often have the ability to link parent and child work items, which allows you to see the rollup of the work in the backlog and the overall plan. Additionally, these tools can provide you with an easy way to track progress and identify any gaps in traceability.

For Jira, tagging the Feature for every User Story is sooo helpful in organizing everything. Jira can talk to Confluence too when you're in and out of Scrum Docs or PRDs, making a glimpse of User Stories really helpful to reference. I know Atlassian has some good articles on how to start doing this, Google may be your best friend here. Key thing is to be consistent though

Also ChatGPT has been really amazing when asking questions and trying to brainstorm with (e.g. for me it's nearly like having a 2nd person to talk ideas through --- just be cautious to avoid any IP or anything in it)

Also, I pick up a lot of this knowledge pretty easily and quickly but then I forget it as soon as I'm done with a project, so I don't really remember how VPC provisioning works, for example, even though I used to know.

Hello all, happy Sunday! A week from now I'll be starting a new role as a TPM. I was previously a PgM (no T, although I did work with a tech team) at Amazon and am starting at a new company after being laid off earlier this year.

For those who have successfully made the transition from Program Manager to Technical Program Manager, I'd love to hear your best advice! 😊 Thank you!
was trying to search for answers to similar questions we've answered in the past.. couldn't find it..

some quick tips.. this may apply to any transition, so pls use what might be relevant for you: 
1. understand what the company expects from TPMs. This may be codified in career frameworks for TPMs, if not your manager is your best bet. Also ask for an onboarding buddy if you are not assigned one automatically. 
2. try and attend the basic engineering bootcamp/onboarding program if they have one. if they don't, find a couple of newish engineers and see what they did to get onboarded. you might get lucky and get a gdoc with a bunch of reading material that could have some great detail. 
3. start understanding the tech behind the product/feature you will be primarily supporting. you should build a basic understanding by reading thru existing docs. and then find engineers who are working on this product (hopefully there's an onboarding doc from your manager with people to meet). in the initial conversation, maybe a good idea to talk to the eng and say "this is what I understand about the product and the tech stack" - what else can you tell me about it.. continue to build the picture based on every single conversation you have from there. 
4. constantly check with your manager how you are doing w.r.t expectations

I didn't make the transition but I did work as at amazon as a TPM. You can expect to be a little overwhelmed with the tech stack and amount of knowledge of the developers working in the space - that's perfectly normal. I suggest to not try and drink the whole thing in on day 1. Work with the manager pr PM to identify an area where they need help, ask the devs working in that space to spend a little bit of time with you and give you a high level arch overview, ask for some documentation on how things work (amazon has a lot of great documentation, it's part of the culture) and define some near term goals you'd like to achieve. You can't and won't be a tech expert in a day but you can become knowledgeable enough in a small space to start contributing quickly.
Thanks @AG and @CaliMan !! Really appreciate your advice. Just to clarify, I worked at Amazon for most of my career up to this point, but I'm going to a different company now 😊

Hey ya’ll. Started a new job a few months ago (MANGA) and getting dinged regularly on my (written) executive communication skills.  (Too much detail to not enough detail via email). Basically “explain the situation to someone who knows nothing about the day to day” type stuff. If anyone has resources to learn how to get better at this I would be immensely grateful. (Senior / Mid Career. Haven’t had problems with this in the past but I’ve generally worked startups not corporate.)

Own the Room is a great training. I do have a communication/reporting template that aims to provide a way to reach different audience levels. And you can also leverage chatgpt to help you craft emails that give you a good balance on details vs exec summary.

Mental model that I personally use for my reporting:
1) Understand the level of details your stakeholders care about.
2) be mindful on not to show that you know “things”. It adds unnecessary details.
3) Curate the narratives reading it few times. Ask yourself if it addresses what you want to communicate?
4) use bullets, bold letters or even appropriate emoji to draw attention of the readers
5) Think what questions will pop up in your reader’s mind reading your narrative and answer those, if possible. If not, share your plan to figure out the answers.
6) Observe other’s writings. Find role mode who you can follow.

This. especially (1) and (6). Put yourself in the exec audience shoes and think through what they might care about, what their worries, desires are. What would make them look good, get them fired etc. And then write in a way that concisely speaks to that. 

A good framework is  -- What has happened? So what / implications? Now what / next steps?

Weave in timelines and if you're on track or not. If at risk, what mitigations are planned, what help is needed from who, what decisions need to be made, what you are recommending based on analyzed trade-offs etc.

Assume execs know the why and motivation already as they sponsored the program.

"How" is not really relevant to an exec audience in most cases unless there's a decision to be made that involves significant investment.

I think it’s helpful to require RAG status for each workstream along with a path-to-green for at risk or off track programs

- A good project plan makes reporting easier, the status updates typically reflect progress against said plan
- Link to supporting docs owned by other teams to ensure they’re keeping their docs and representation of state current
- A high level Gantt chart milestones across workstreams and a “we are here” line helps
- Lastly, any metrics however crude that convey the story help
Also @Lucy Goose you can add a section “call for action “ so that the exec can focus on the issues/ programs that need his/her intervention 1st followed by rest of the updates.

Grew up poor. Feel free to reach out on intake process.... i created matrix to evaluate project/program for assigning either TPM or assigning TPM self-serve package. Requesting team had to submit TPM request form consisting below mentioned information.

2 - OKR/Success Criteria

There was a question about handling the intake process for an ongoing program in the meeting today.  I am sharing the process I have set up. 

I have established the intake process for Quote-To-Cash systems.  Cross-functional teams submit their requests through JIRA, which has been customized with the following fields:
- Requesting Organization
- Business Justification (a business requirements document is expected for large projects)
- Revenue Impact (expected revenue from new business/cost of non-compliance)
- Requested Delivery Quarter
- Number of Organizations/Systems Impacted
- ….some more…

TPM  holds regular intake meetings where all tickets are reviewed. The requestor presents their request, and the product, engineering, QA, and cross-functional teams review and seek clarifications about the ticket.

Tickets are then prioritized based on several factors, such as business criticality and quick wins ets.  The dev team commits to the tickets accordingly.
All committed tickets for each quarter are published in the Wiki as JIRA dashboards.

Committed tickets go through development, QA cycles, and deployments. The status of each phase, along with committed dates, is updated in JIRA to ensure transparency for the requestor.

Please feel free to reach out to me for more details.

There are few I can think of - 
1. R&D Programs - Initiatives focused on innovation and new product areas.

Meeting a senior product leader for the first time. Wondering what can be some of the go-to questions to uncover their expectations.
I would like to know answer from others for this question- How will you use your technical background while working with the engineers as a TPM?

My personal approach would be to outline what I think TPMs are supposed to do, then tie that back to the question. My default description is that TPMs are expected     
1) Align goals, milestones, and KPIs
2) Proactively identify and mitigate risk
3) Communicate those details upward, downward, and sideways. 

To align goals and milestones with engineers, I use my technical background to make sure we're aligning on the best solution that meets our business needs. For example, if the business has a hard calendar deadline, I might probe the estimates provided by engineering to see if they're gold-plating and overengineering. On the other hand, if we've been suffering from poor quality, I might drive a discussion with engineering to understand what tech debt we need to invest in (integration tests, health checks, monitoring), and advocate for more investment with leadership.

To proactively identify and mitigate risk, I use my technical background to build a deep understanding of the architecture we're developing and asking questions to ensure we're building the solution that meets our business needs. For example, I would raise concerns if there seems to be single points of failure in our design. As a TPM, I also often have a broader view and can connect dots in disparate sections of the organization (e.g. "Hey, you're proposing to update this manifest file, but have you talked to this partner that consumes that manifest file downstream?"

Lastly, I use my technical background to accurately represent engineering's work in communications to our partners and stakeholders, or vice versa. 

(Specific examples are ideal if you've got them. You can edit how deeply or broadly you take this depending on how much time you have in the interview. I might present the 3 objectives and ask if the interviewer is particularly interested in 1 of them to dive into)

Hi Folks, I've just signed a job offer for a Sr TPM role which will be my first time changing company in the 10 years since I graduated. Does anyone have any best practices or general words of advice on learning the new domain, handling the culture change, and ramping up effectively?

In addition:
Read all the documentation. If it's wrong or non-existent, help write some of it down.

Depending on the domain, I've made it a point to sign into site down war room calls so I can learn what problems are causing outages.

Respect what came before: It's really easy to come into a new company and find things that look completely screwed up. Recognize that there's usually some good reasons for why things got to be the way they are. (That doesn't mean you give up on change, just don't flip tables at how dumb you think stuff is). common example is why the org is still running a monolith instead of a cleaner micro service based architecture.

I mean, nothing is ever really hopeless. What's your background that got you domain expertise in Compute HW? It sounds like you already understand the basic SW concepts? (e.g. What's an API? What's a Front End vs a Back End? )

Honestly , it's not TPM's job to always ferret out all the unknown unknowns. It's helpful if TPMs can, but certainly not mandatory. 

I've found engineers often respond well to prompting, things like "Where do we think we have single points of failure? At what point do we need to do something about that?" Or "What's our biggest scaling bottleneck?" Then you build up your sense of risks

That said, the resource I use as a refresher is Gaurav Sen's videos. He has paid stuff now, but the free stuff is good https://youtube.com/playlist?list=PLMCXHnjXnTnvo6alSjVkgxV-VH6EPyvoX&si=ifW2swkqOiyV2Y4P

Once you hit Principal TPM, the next steps aren't always clearly defined. People usually take one of three paths:

1. People management - moving into Manager or Director roles. These are limited, but the best way in is to start showing leadership beyond your immediate team and make your interest clear. I have seen some very technical TPMs transitioning to SDM roles as well.
2. Lateral moves - some transition into Product or Strategy roles, especially if they're already influencing roadmaps or working across functions.
3. Deep IC path - this is extremely rare, but some companies offer higher-level IC roles like Director or Architect or Distinguished TPM that focus on large-scale, technical problem-solving.

Question for TPM Managers. What makes a TPM candidate to stand out among all applicants? any stories about a truly outstanding candidate you have met?

1/ Taking ownership of (technical) problems and owning the outcome even when it's "not my job" and 2/ Ability to establish personal and team/org-wide accountability are hallmarks of the best TPMs I've worked with. TPMs who do this naturally gain influence and trust that enables them to have outsized/scaled impact.

* depth and breadth of experience, as demonstrated thru their stories (and answers to follow-up questions) 
* being confident in their skills and experiences such that the interview flows more like a conversation. 
* fully owns up to their successes _and_ failures. 
* isn't afraid to push back on the interviewer (but this needs a mature interviewer to appreciate) 
* has done their research on the company/role/interviewer and asks very good questions
could you give an example of some very good questions?

questions that are researched based on the interviewer / company / product area .. 
"I heard the CEO talk about X in the last earnings call - and looks like you work in that space. What are some of the key challenges and solutions y'all are working thru these days?"

This is a pretty lengthy subject, but my general MO is to frame proposals as What?, So What?, and So Now What?

You identified a What "We have a lot of untriaged bugs".

Now answer So What? Why does your team care about these? That will help guide your triage process and next steps. Is the team worried this is hiding UX fit and finish? Are there big security issues hiding among them? Is there tech debt that is a ticking time bomb and will lead to site down issues? 

We don't write code for funsies, and the same is true of fixing tickets. 

Put another way, you need an answer for: "These bugs have been around and the world hasn't fallen over. What's the problem?"

Options include 
a) big bankruptcy 
b) use ai to summarize and use that as a signal to prioritize/ deprioritize direction 
c) pebble category for small fixes. 
d) explicitly decommit 
e)

The issue is also that many bugs are triaged, but they are not acting upon, or need more information to be properly triaged or then acted upon to be closed in a satisfactory state. To avoid losing user trust, overall improve UX, even improve code and product design.

Do you all specific policies or templates that you have seen gain traction in your work to help reduce the size of that bug mountain? And prevent the bug mountain from re appearing? AI is definitely one way, but I’m trying to source other tactical and strategic approaches that can better help and inform any AI models and overall processes.

One effective approach is to treat genuine system defects—those truly impacting customers—with a top-down strategy. This starts with aligning product, engineering, and reserve teams on capacity allocation for defects alongside feature work, based on data-driven insights.
Conduct regular operational reviews with leadership to discuss key trends, such as:
•    Created vs. closed defects, tracked month-over-month.
•    Backlog trends, including the number of open defects at any given time (e.g., over 3 months, 6 months, etc.).
Establish clear SLAs, for example:
•    P0 defects should not remain open for more than 1 day.
•    P1 defects should not exceed 15 days.
Additionally, for defects lingering in the system for over a year have senior people  evaluate whether they represent actual issues. This cleanup prevents unnecessary noise in the backlog.

Based on the SOTA agentic engineering (e.g. overnight loops of Claude Code / Codex) you should be able to audit the actual code base against each JIRA ticket.

You could also beta test (in your own local sandbox) to see if the agents could make the fixes and PRs themselves, but at minimum you can optimally triage each JIRA against the actual code.

IMHO, this is the critical bottleneck. The TPM can drive prioritization clarity and with minimal engineering effort per sprint build an automated agentic bug pipeline with adjustable thresholds to either create the PR directly or pre-flight the stated problem + likely solutions.
